title: Beyond Linear: A Whirlwind Tour of Vector Transformations & Projections
subtitle: From rotation matrices to fiber bundles — every way vectors can be transformed, projected, and embedded

children: This tutorial is a whirlwind tour. We start with familiar linear transformations and end up in hyperbolic space and fiber bundles. The goal is
— knowing what each transformation does, why it exists, and when you'd use it.

children.children: You already know these — a linear transformation T: ℝⁿ → ℝᵐ is multiplication by a matrix. Every linear map preserves the origin and is fully determined by what it does to basis vectors.

children: by angle θ:

children.children: R(θ) = [[cos θ, −sin θ], [sin θ, cos θ]]

children: — preserves lengths and angles (orthogonal, det = 1).
— stretches/compresses along axes. Uniform if sx = sy.
— slides layers proportionally. Parallelograms stay parallelograms.
— flips across an axis. Orthogonal with det = −1.

children.children: Key insight: ANY 2×2 matrix is some combination of rotation, scaling, and reflection. That's exactly what the SVD (Singular Value Decomposition) tells you: A = UΣVᵀ = rotate · scale · rotate.
A linear map must satisfy T(0) = 0. So the origin is always fixed. To move the origin, you need something beyond linearity — that's where affine transformations come in (Section 3).
Projection is the most important linear operation you'll encounter. It answers: 'What's the closest point in a subspace to my vector?'
A shadow cast by a vertical light onto the floor is an orthogonal projection onto the floor plane. The shadow is the 'best approximation' of you that lives entirely in 2D.

children: To project vector
onto the direction of

children.children: The projection matrix onto the column space of B is P = B(BᵀB)⁻¹Bᵀ. This is idempotent: P² = P.

When you solve Ax = b by least squares (x̂ = (AᵀA)⁻¹Aᵀb), you're projecting b onto the column space of A. The residual b − Ax̂ is orthogonal to that column space. This is the geometric heart of linear regression.

Orthogonal projections use the perpendicular direction. Oblique projections drop the vector along a different (non-perpendicular) direction onto the subspace. Both are idempotent (P² = P), but oblique projections aren't symmetric. They appear in numerical methods and biorthogonal wavelet transforms.
An affine transformation is linear + translation: T(x) = Ax + b. This is the bread and butter of computer graphics and neural network layers.

children: Pure linear transformations can't translate (they fix the origin). Affine maps break free: they can shift, rotate, scale, and shear all at once.
Embed 2D point (x, y) as (x, y, 1) in 3D. Now translation becomes matrix multiplication:

children.children: By lifting to one higher dimension, we've linearized translation! This is a recurring theme: make something nonlinear linear by adding dimensions.
Every neural network layer (before the activation) is an affine transformation: y = Wx + b. The nonlinearity (ReLU, etc.) is what makes it more than affine. We'll come back to this.

Projective transformations are the most general transformations that map lines to lines. They're what your camera does when it photographs the world — parallel railroad tracks converge to a vanishing point.

If affine transformations are like looking at a painting straight-on (parallel lines stay parallel), projective transformations are like looking at it from an angle — the painting appears to recede, parallel lines converge, and distant things shrink more than close things.

children: In projective space, a point is represented by an equivalence class of vectors: (x, y, w) ~ (λx, λy, λw) for any λ ≠ 0. To recover 2D coordinates: (x/w, y/w).

children.children: A projective transformation (homography) is any invertible 3×3 matrix H applied to these homogeneous coordinates. With 8 degrees of freedom (9 entries minus 1 for scale), it can map any quadrilateral to any other quadrilateral.

children: Those are 'points at infinity' — the directions where parallel lines meet. Projective space makes 'infinity' a first-class citizen.

children.children: A vanishing point is where w → 0: a family of parallel lines converge. Each set of parallel lines has its own vanishing point, and these form a 'line at infinity.'

The cross-ratio of four collinear points (A,B,C,D) = (AC·BD)/(BC·AD) is the ONE quantity preserved by projective transformations. While distances, ratios, and even parallelism are destroyed, the cross-ratio survives.

children: Hierarchy of 2D transformations (each includes the previous):
(3 DOF: rotation + translation) ⊂
(4 DOF: + uniform scale) ⊂
(6 DOF: + non-uniform scale, shear) ⊂
(8 DOF: + perspective)

children.children: Now we break free from linearity entirely. Lines no longer need to map to lines; the world gets wild.

children: Take a 2D point (x, y) and lift it to a higher-dimensional space: φ(x, y) = (x², √2·xy, y², x, y, 1). Now something that was a circle in 2D (nonlinear boundary) becomes a
in 6D (linear boundary)!
The

: you never actually compute φ(x). Instead, you notice that φ(x)·φ(y) = (x·y + 1)² — a simple formula in the original space. Kernel methods (SVMs, Gaussian processes) exploit this: work in a huge feature space without paying for it.

props.title: About "Instead, you notice that φ(x)·φ(y) = (x·y + 1)² — a simple f..."

children: The key insight is that dot products in the high-dimensional feature space can be computed using only the original low-dimensional inputs — you never actually build the expensive transformed vectors. So when an SVM needs to measure similarity between two data points (which is all dot products really are), it just plugs the original coordinates into (x·y + 1)² instead of mapping to 6D and dotting there. This is why kernel methods scale to infinite-dimensional feature spaces (like Gaussian/RBF kernels): if the dot product has a closed-form shortcut
, the dimensionality of φ's output literally doesn't matter.

props.title: Q: Does this mean that no matter how complex the formula that lifts x and y to higher-dimensional space, their distance in that space will always be (x dot y + 1)^2?

children.children: No — (x·y + 1)² is the shortcut for one specific kernel, the degree-2 polynomial kernel. Different lifting maps get different shortcuts. The Gaussian/RBF kernel, for instance, uses exp(-||x - y||² / 2σ²), which corresponds to a lifting into an infinite-dimensional space. A degree-3 polynomial kernel would be (x·y + 1)³. Each kernel function IS its own closed-form shortcut for its own particular high-dimensional mapping.

What "closed-form shortcut" means here is that for any given kernel, there exists a simple formula you can evaluate on the original low-dimensional inputs that gives you the exact same answer as if you had done the full expensive lifting and then computed the dot product up there. The miracle is not that one formula works for all liftings — it's that for each lifting, such a compact formula exists at all.

Think of it like travel distances. If you want the driving distance between two cities, you'd normally need to trace every road. But for straight-line distance, you have the Pythagorean formula — a closed-form shortcut. A different notion of distance (say, walking time) would need a different shortcut formula. The kernel trick is the same idea: each type of high-dimensional similarity has its own compact formula, and you pick the kernel that matches the feature space you care about.

children: Each layer of a neural network is:
— an affine transformation followed by a pointwise nonlinearity σ (ReLU, tanh, etc.).

children.children: This combination is extraordinarily powerful. With ReLU, each layer partitions space into regions (one side is zeroed out, the other is linear). A deep network builds up an increasingly complex piecewise-linear map.

children: Connection to your MLP linearization work: at any specific input x₀, the network IS locally linear (Jacobian = product of weight matrices × diagonal ReLU masks). The 'linearized MLP' captures what the network does in that neighborhood.

children.children: A diffeomorphism is a smooth, invertible, differentiable map with a smooth inverse. Think of it as 'bending space without tearing or gluing.' Normalizing flows in ML use chains of diffeomorphisms to transform a simple distribution (Gaussian) into a complex one.

Imagine a rubber sheet. You can stretch, compress, and warp it, but never rip it or fold it over itself. That's a diffeomorphism. Every small region still looks topologically the same — just distorted.
A manifold is a space that locally looks like ℝⁿ but globally can be curved. The surface of the Earth is a 2D manifold embedded in 3D — locally flat (your backyard), globally curved (the planet).

A manifold is like a patchwork quilt: each patch looks like a flat piece of fabric (ℝⁿ), but they're sewn together into something curved. The 'atlas' of patches with 'transition maps' between them is exactly the mathematical definition.
At each point p on a manifold, the tangent space TₚM is the flat vector space that best approximates the manifold at p. For the sphere at the north pole, it's the horizontal plane touching the top.

children: (exp_p): takes a tangent vector v ∈ TₚM and 'shoots' it along the manifold to produce a point. On a sphere, exp sends a tangent vector along the great circle in that direction.
(log_p): the inverse — takes a point q on the manifold and returns the tangent vector at p that points toward q. This is how you compute 'directions' on curved spaces.

children.children: Place a sphere on a plane, and draw a line from the north pole through any point on the sphere. Where that line hits the plane is the stereographic projection of that point.

children: Key properties: (1)
— preserves angles (but not distances). (2) The north pole maps to infinity. (3) Circles on the sphere map to circles (or lines) on the plane.
Formula: for a point (x, y, z) on the unit sphere, stereographic projection from the north pole gives (

children.children: A geodesic is the shortest path between two points on a manifold — the curved-space equivalent of a straight line. On a sphere, geodesics are great circles (think airplane routes). On a hyperboloid, they're hyperbolas.

This matters for ML: if your data lives on a manifold (and high-dimensional data often does), straight-line interpolation in ambient space might cut through regions that don't exist on the manifold. Geodesic interpolation stays on the surface.
What happens when we change the metric — the very definition of 'distance'? We leave Euclidean space and enter strange, beautiful worlds.
Hyperbolic space has constant negative curvature. It's the geometry where Euclid's parallel postulate fails dramatically — through a point not on a line, there are INFINITELY many parallel lines.

children: : the entire infinite hyperbolic plane is squeezed into a unit disk. Points near the boundary are 'infinitely far away' — distances grow exponentially as you approach the edge. The metric is

children.children: Why ML cares about hyperbolic space:

children: Trees and hierarchies have exponential growth — the number of nodes at depth d grows as 2^d. Euclidean space grows polynomially (volume ~ r^n). Hyperbolic space grows exponentially (volume ~ e^r). This means tree-like data embeds naturally in hyperbolic space with far fewer dimensions than Euclidean space. Poincaré embeddings can represent entire WordNet hierarchies in just 5 dimensions!
: represents hyperbolic space as the upper sheet of a hyperboloid in Minkowski space with metric
. Computationally more stable than the Poincaré model — most practical implementations use this.

children.children: On a sphere, 'lines' are great circles. ALL lines intersect (no parallels!). The angles of a triangle sum to MORE than 180°. The excess is proportional to the area.
Spherical embeddings are used when data has directional structure: word embeddings normalized to unit length, von Mises-Fisher distributions, and directional statistics all live on spheres.

children: A Möbius transformation of the complex plane is
where ad − bc ≠ 0. These are the conformal (angle-preserving) automorphisms of the Riemann sphere.

children.children: They map circles to circles (or lines), generalize rotations and inversions, and form a group (SL(2,ℂ)/±I = PSL(2,ℂ)). In the Poincaré disk model, isometries of hyperbolic space ARE Möbius transformations that map the disk to itself.

The three classical geometries — Euclidean (zero curvature), spherical (positive curvature), and hyperbolic (negative curvature) — are the three possible constant-curvature spaces. Everything else is a blend.
How do we faithfully represent high-dimensional structure in lower dimensions? Different methods preserve different things.

children: preserve ALL distances. Going from ℝⁿ to ℝᵐ (m < n) while preserving all distances is generally impossible — but approximately possible!
preserve angles but not distances. Stereographic projection is conformal. Mercator projection is conformal. These are great when direction matters more than magnitude.
preserve areas but distort shapes. The equal-area (Lambert) projection is one.

children.children: You can't have it all — this is a fundamental tension in differential geometry (Theorema Egregium: you can't flatten a sphere without distorting something).

children: : orthogonal projection onto the subspace of maximum variance. Linear, global, preserves large pairwise distances. It IS the best linear projection in the least-squares sense.

: nonlinear. Converts distances to probabilities, then minimizes KL divergence between high-D and low-D probability distributions. Preserves local neighborhoods beautifully but distorts global structure.

: builds a topological representation (fuzzy simplicial complex) in high-D and finds a low-D analog. Preserves more global structure than t-SNE, faster, and has actual mathematical foundations in algebraic topology.
The JL lemma says: for n points in high-D space, a
projection to O(log n / ε²) dimensions preserves all pairwise distances to within a factor of (1 ± ε). No optimization needed — just random matrices!

children.children: This is astonishing: 1 million points in 10,000 dimensions can be projected to ~200 dimensions while approximately preserving all distances. It's used in locality-sensitive hashing, compressed sensing, and random features for kernel approximation.

children: This section is advanced and brief. It's here because these ideas are reshaping geometric deep learning.
A
is a space that locally looks like a product B × F (base space × fiber), but globally might be twisted. Think of a Möbius strip: locally it's a rectangle, but globally it has a twist.

children.children: Imagine a hairy ball. At each point on the sphere (base space), there's a tangent plane (fiber). The tangent bundle of the sphere is a fiber bundle. The 'hairy ball theorem' says you can't comb the hair flat without creating a cowlick — this is topology constraining geometry.

children: A
is a change of basis (reference frame) that varies from point to point on the base space. In physics, gauge invariance is the principle that laws shouldn't depend on your local coordinate choice.

children.children: Modern geometric deep learning (Bronstein et al.'s 'Geometric Deep Learning' blueprint) frames neural network architectures in terms of symmetries and equivariance:

children: are equivariant to translations (the same filter works everywhere)
are equivariant to permutations
work on manifolds where there's no global coordinate system — they use 'gauge transformations' (local frame changes) and demand the network output doesn't depend on the choice of frame

children.children: The fiber bundle viewpoint unifies all of these: features are 'sections' of a fiber bundle, and equivariance means the network commutes with gauge transformations. This is the deepest connection between physics and deep learning.

children: Here's the hierarchy of transformations we've covered, from most to least structured:
(preserve distances) ⊂
(preserve angles) ⊂
(preserve origin + linearity) ⊂
(+ translation) ⊂
(lines → lines) ⊂
(smooth + invertible) ⊂
(topology only)

children.children: And orthogonally: flat spaces (Euclidean) → constant curvature (spherical, hyperbolic) → general manifolds → fiber bundles.

The recurring theme: when something is nonlinear, lift to a higher dimension where it becomes linear (homogeneous coordinates, kernel trick, feature maps). When space is curved, work in tangent spaces where it's locally flat. When there's no global coordinate system, demand your operations be gauge-equivariant. Geometry is the language of structure.
